skill.name: azure-identity-dotnet
skill.description: Guidance for using Azure Identity with .NET applications, including DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, service principals, credential chains, and Azure SDK clients.
skill.summary: Implement Microsoft Entra authentication for Azure SDK clients in .NET.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, network, environment variable, certificate, and weak crypto patterns. Review found these are Markdown documentation examples, placeholder Azure credential names, and reference links, not executable skill behavior or data exfiltration. The skill is safe to publish with a low-risk note because it teaches authentication patterns that users must apply with normal secret handling controls.

low_findings.title: Markdown examples flagged as command execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The detected command patterns are package installation examples and inline SDK names in SKILL.md. They are documentation text and are not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are inside Markdown code fences or table cells. No script file, runtime hook, or instruction to run arbitrary user input is present.

low_findings.title: Azure credential variables documented as placeholders
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The environment variable names are standard Azure Identity configuration examples. The skill does not read, print, transmit, or store these values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholders such as client ID, tenant ID, secret, and certificate path. There is no evidence of credential access beyond documentation.

low_findings.title: Azure URLs are examples and references
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are sample Azure resource endpoints and documentation links. They do not define outbound requests made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in C# snippets and a reference table. No evidence found of hidden network calls or exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Azure PowerShell names flagged as invocation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The PowerShell references are Azure.Identity credential type names and documentation table entries. They are not PowerShell commands launched by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is an Azure credential chain and C# class reference. No command string, subprocess launch, or shell execution path exists.

low_findings.title: Sensitive authentication examples require normal safeguards
low_findings.description: LOW_RISK: The skill includes examples for client secrets, certificate files, and diagnostic logging. These are legitimate Azure Identity topics, but users should avoid committing secrets and should review logs for sensitive data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are valid SDK usage and contain placeholders, but the topic area involves secrets and certificates. The concern is user misuse, not malicious skill behavior.

content.value_statement: Azure authentication code can be hard to choose and configure correctly. This skill gives focused guidance for Azure.Identity credentials, credential chains, and production-ready .NET patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, service principal credentials, and developer credentials.
Provides .NET examples for Azure SDK clients that use TokenCredential implementations.
Shows environment variable configuration for Azure service principals and certificates.
Covers dependency injection setup with Microsoft.Extensions.Azure.
Summarizes credential chain behavior, sovereign cloud configuration, retry options, and common exceptions.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not authenticate to Azure for the user.
It does not verify tenant IDs, client IDs, secrets, certificates, or role assignments.
Version numbers may become stale after Azure SDK releases.
It does not replace organization-specific identity, compliance, or key management policies.

use_cases.title: Add Azure SDK authentication
use_cases.description: Choose the correct Azure.Identity credential and connect a .NET application to Azure SDK clients.

use_cases.title: Move from local auth to production identity
use_cases.description: Replace development credentials with managed identity or deterministic production credentials.

use_cases.title: Review Azure authentication patterns
use_cases.description: Check credential chain choices, environment configuration, retry settings, and error handling before release.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a credential
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an Azure SDK client.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose an Azure.Identity credential for a .NET app that connects to Azure Storage. Explain the simplest safe option for development and production.

prompt_templates.title: Configure managed identity
prompt_templates.scenario: Production authentication for an Azure-hosted workload.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show how to use ManagedIdentityCredential in a .NET service. Include when to use system-assigned and user-assigned managed identities.

prompt_templates.title: Build a credential chain
prompt_templates.scenario: Custom authentication order for mixed environments.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a ChainedTokenCredential for a .NET app that should use managed identity in Azure and Azure CLI during local development.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an identity implementation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of existing authentication code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure.Identity approach for production readiness. Check credential determinism, secret handling, logging, sovereign cloud settings, and exception handling.

output_examples.input: Which credential should I use for an Azure Functions app in production?
output_examples.output: Use managed identity when possible. Prefer a specific ManagedIdentityCredential in production, and avoid relying on the full developer credential chain.

output_examples.input: How should I configure a service principal for local testing?
output_examples.output: Use standard Azure Identity environment variables with placeholder values replaced locally. Keep secrets out of source control and prefer managed identity in hosted environments.

output_examples.input: What should I check when DefaultAzureCredential fails?
output_examples.output: Check tenant configuration, credential availability, role assignments, managed identity status, and whether the selected credential is excluded by options.

best_practices: Use managed identity for Azure-hosted production workloads whenever possible.
Use deterministic credentials in production instead of a broad developer credential chain.
Keep secrets, certificates, and diagnostic logs out of source control and shared outputs.
anti_patterns: Do not commit client secrets, certificate passwords, or local credential files.
Do not enable interactive browser authentication in unattended production services.
Do not assume DefaultAzureCredential selects the same credential in every environment.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples for Azure.Identity in .NET.

faq.question: Can it create Azure identities or role assignments?
faq.answer: No. It can explain what to configure, but you must create identities and permissions in Azure.

faq.question: Is DefaultAzureCredential safe for production?
faq.answer: It can work, but production services often benefit from a specific credential such as ManagedIdentityCredential.

faq.question: Does it support Microsoft Entra ID?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is for Azure SDK authentication through Microsoft Entra ID.

faq.question: Can it help with sovereign clouds?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for setting Azure authority hosts such as Azure Government and Azure China.

faq.question: Does it include current package versions?
faq.answer: It lists package versions from the skill text, but users should verify current versions before release.
